posts.name: Kelby James Russell
posts.cooked:
When 1987 is your vintage, you get a lot of sad looks from wine folks thinking about birth year bottles. Up to now I’ve had one or two German Rieslings (including a stellar Eiswein from Selbach Oster), but any '87 is hard to come by. Let alone a dry table wine, a red, or a Grand Cru Burgundy.

Was prepared for this to be adequate and simply a fun wine to have access to, we tend to be pretty unsentimental wine drinkers in that way, but it was actually remarkable. Needed decanting for the purpose of opening up (!), after which the spice, bramble, and purple fruit came right out. Rich and structured palate with more earthiness that showed no real signs of its age, with a velvety finish of red fruit and cigar smoke. A top wine that I’ve ever had the pleasure to enjoy, and a great pairing with the Christmas Goose I cooked up.

BC455101-6CF4-4707-B910-1DB94675F1E81920×2400 178 KB

Cheers and happy holidays to you all!

posts.name: Alan Rath
posts.cooked: Glad it was good! Our wedding year, so I have kind of the same problem. Well made Napa cabs can be quite good, like Montelena, Mondavi, Dunn, etc.

posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked: Try Mondavi reserve 1987

posts.name: Jim Anderson
posts.cooked: Several years ago I had a bottle of Dujac 1987 Bonnes Mares. Granted, this was not just a couple of years ago and well before they increased their holdings there so it was a pretty special wine. At about 20 years of ago it was drinking very nicely if not a top notch example of Bonnes Mares which, in the moment, that was quite nice. Glad your bottle did right by you and exceeded expectations.

posts.name: Kelby James Russell
posts.cooked: Indeed, with any bottle that old it always comes down to a bit of luck how the cork is and how the wine was stored (which I had nothing to do with until it came to me a couple years ago). Was really delighted by the entire thing, although it did convince me to finally buy a Durand opener.

posts.name: Kelby James Russell
posts.cooked: yes to this and @Mark_Golodetz suggestion - I’ve heard Napa '87s are worth seeking out.

posts.name: Jeremy Holmes
posts.cooked: Well done Kelby. Many pleasant surprises to be had with so called ‘off year’ Burgundy.

posts.name: John Maguire
posts.cooked:
Kelby_James_Russell:

yes to this and @Mark_Golodetz suggestion - I’ve heard Napa '87s are worth seeking out.

“87 Napa — now that’s a name I haven’t heard in a very long time….” — Obi Wan Cabernet.
I haven’t had an 87 Napa cab in a quite a while (15+ years) but when I did they were outstanding.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: Georges Lignier had an interlude of making very interesting wines in the mid-1980s. The 1985s in particular are actually brilliant. After that, things went awry; today, the new generation has changed/fixed a lot, without seeking any limelight, but they remain quite under the radar.

posts.name: Joshua Kates
posts.cooked: I’ve had a good luck with G. Lignier, especially their Clos St. Denis. Maybe not top of the line GC, but the '90 definitely was a lovely and memorable wine.

posts.name: Thomas Keim
posts.cooked: 1987 Was a terrific vintage for Cabernet Sauvignon in Northern California - Also a sleeper vintage for many upper tier Bordeaux (LaMission Haut-Brion is wonderful - as was Pichon Lalande) and because of the “bad vintage” press, are still pretty cheap -

posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked: I opened a bottle of 2015 Bonnes Mares recently, as I had half a dozen bottles and I wanted to see the progression. Extremely promising once it opened.i will wait another ten years, but figure that this will be a rewarding purchase.

posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked:
Thomas_Keim:

1987 Was a terrific vintage for Cabernet Sauvignon in Northern California - Also a sleeper vintage for many upper tier Bordeaux (LaMission Haut-Brion is wonderful - as was Pichon Lalande) and because of the “bad vintage” press, are still pretty cheap -

I think you might be in the wrong thread.

posts.name: Alan Rath
posts.cooked: The thread kind of expanded to general 87 recs. My fault lol.

posts.name: Michael Baryla
posts.cooked:
I was able to buy a lot of the Denis directly from distribution and have a huge problem with bottle variation. Mostly 2012,13,14 - out of 750s and mags.

Some of them have been absolutely fantastic and others are just dull and uninspiring. No TCA/cork issues just very bland wine.

I’ve been offered their bottling practices as explanation but not sure how accurate that is.

posts.name: Joel D@vid$on
posts.cooked:
My year also. Lucky for me, a friend of mine bought a ton of 87 burgs on release. Most have been quite wonderful. Including the 1987 Georges Lignier Clos St Denis. This was actually the consensus WOTN at a burg night last year.

We also just did a very fun tasting with some great ones, including 1987 Rousseau Clos De Beze. I’ll have to post a writeup soon.

PS, when they’re on, the 87 Montelena and 87 Spottswoode are stellar.

posts.name: Kelby James Russell
posts.cooked: That sort of variation is my worst nightmare as a winemaker sending wines out into the world. My default assumption would be sub-threshold TCA suppressing aromas, but if there haven’t been any outright corked bottles that is just plain… weird.

posts.name: Kelby James Russell
posts.cooked:
Fascinating - was it a push towards more oak/extraction in the 1990s, which is my default assumption for where folks got pushed to in that era before reigning it back in?

Also, the lesson here from folks like @Thomas_Keim @JoelD @Alan_Rath is clearly that I need to keep my eyes open for other 1987s. Some really good wines and fair values still to be had, an increasingly rare combination.

posts.name: Michael Baryla
posts.cooked:
Who knows. I haven’t been to that domaine.
Are there still producers bottling from barrel? Could be barrel variation.

I’m sure there are still producers (no idea on Lignier) that use slow bottling lines essentially bottling a tank for a year with varying headspace.

Storage can obviously be an issue but these were all directly from the distributor/importer here in CA and in my cellar together so doubtful.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: No, I think it was bad agronomy. Too much oak and extraction was for the folks who were trying to make better wine Hindsight is harsh…

suggested_topics.title: Cellar Talk
suggested_topics.excerpt: There is just something I enjoy about wine cellars, whether old or new. The subdued light, the coolness, and the combined smells of mold, fermentation, and wine. They hold anticipation, potential, and hope all the while …
